title: 控方证人
summary:
Mr. Radish informs Lady Lancaster that a child once starved to death at House No. 19, and while no one has seen a ghost, some have heard a child crying. Despite these unsettling rumors, Lady Lancaster decides to purchase the house due to its reasonable price. She moves into the residence with her father, Mr. Wimborne, and her son, Jeffrey.

As the family settles in, Mr. Wimborne hears the sound of dragging, painful footsteps on the oak staircase following Jeffrey, though he dismisses the noise as rain. That night, Mr. Wimborne dreams of a city populated only by crying children who weep because he cannot bring "him" to them. Upon waking, he hears heartbreaking sobbing coming from above. He later hears the same sound during the day and discovers that the maid believes Jeffrey is the one crying, even though Jeffrey appears happy. Mr. Wimborne concludes that the sound is produced by the same entity responsible for the dragging footsteps.

Eventually, Jeffrey tells his mother about a lonely, weak little boy living in the attic who watches him play. While Lady Lancaster dismisses the boy as an impossibility, Mr. Wimborne gently encourages Jeffrey to find a way to comfort the child. When Lady Lancaster questions her father's decision to validate the boy's claims, Mr. Wimborne suggests that he and Jeffrey can perceive things she cannot, referencing a question about what lamp might guide children through the darkness.
